title: 📊 Invoicing International Clients: VAT and Tax Rules
description:
Tax and VAT rules when US or UK freelancers invoice clients in other countries in 2026. Reverse charge, withholding, treaty rates, and the platforms that handle compliance.

Personal finance, international invoicing, VAT EU US, freelancer cross border, tax treaty 2026.

segments.voice: Invoicing clients in other countries opens up huge income opportunities for freelancers but also creates real tax complexity. The IRS, HMRC, and EU tax authorities each have their own rules. Get the structure wrong and you can end up paying tax twice on the same income. Today, the practical playbook for cross-border invoicing.

US freelancer invoicing a UK client. From the IRS perspective, the income is taxable in the US regardless of where the client is. From HMRC's perspective, services performed remotely from the US for a UK business are generally outside UK VAT scope under the place of supply rules in section 7A of the UK VAT Act. So no UK VAT applies.

What the UK client needs to do. Most UK businesses paying a US freelancer for services apply the reverse charge mechanism, accounting for VAT in the UK on the value of services received. The freelancer issues a clean invoice with no VAT. The reverse charge note on the invoice helps the UK client process correctly. HMRC's online guidance for B2B services covers the details.

EU client invoicing for US freelancers. Same general principle. Services to EU businesses use the reverse charge under Article forty-four of the EU VAT Directive. The EU client provides their VAT number, you include it on the invoice with a reverse charge note, and you do not collect VAT. The EU client accounts for VAT in their country.

Selling to EU consumers, the OSS scheme. If you sell digital services to EU consumers, the rules differ. You must register for the One Stop Shop or import One Stop Shop schemes to collect EU VAT at the consumer's local rate, then remit through a single EU member state. Most US freelancers selling B2B never need this, but B2C sellers do.

Tax treaties prevent double taxation. The US has tax treaties with around seventy countries. These treaties typically allow a freelancer to claim foreign tax credits on the US return for tax paid abroad, or to claim treaty benefits to reduce foreign withholding. The IRS treaty database at irs.gov lists current rates and provisions.

Form W-8BEN for non-US freelancers receiving US client payments. If you are a non-US person providing services to US clients, you complete Form W-8BEN to claim treaty benefits. Without it, the US client must withhold thirty percent under FATCA rules. Treaty rates vary by country and service type but often drop to zero for B2B services performed remotely.

Form W-9 for US freelancers receiving foreign client payments. From the foreign client's perspective, you may be subject to local withholding. Some countries require US freelancers to obtain a tax residency certificate from the IRS using Form 8802. The certificate is then provided to the foreign client to qualify for reduced withholding under treaty.

Foreign tax credit. If a foreign country withholds tax on your invoice payments, you can claim a foreign tax credit on Form 1116 against your US tax liability. The credit is limited to the US tax on the same foreign-source income. So you generally do not pay double tax, but the calculation gets complex when multiple countries are involved.

Real example, US freelancer with German client. The freelance developer in Austin invoices a German B2B client for ten thousand euros. The German client provides their VAT number. The invoice carries a reverse charge note. No German VAT applies to the freelancer. The Texas-based developer reports the converted USD amount on Schedule C and pays US income and SE tax on it.

Another example, UK freelancer with Australian client. The UK freelancer invoices an Australian client. Australian GST does not apply to services exported from the UK to Australia. The UK freelancer issues a clean invoice with no VAT and reports the income on UK Self Assessment. Australia does not generally withhold tax on service payments to UK residents under the UK-Australia tax treaty.

Currency conversion and timing. The IRS requires reporting income in US dollars at the exchange rate when received. The official IRS yearly average rates are published annually. Most freelancers use Wise, formerly TransferWise, or a multi-currency bank account like Mercury that handles conversion at near-spot rates. Bank wire conversions tend to lose two to four percent.

Wise multi-currency account. Wise lets US, UK, and EU freelancers receive payments in local currency without converting until needed. So a US freelancer can hold euros, pounds, and dollars in separate buckets and convert only when the rate is favorable. This often saves two to four percent compared to traditional bank conversion fees.

Stripe Atlas for international structuring. Stripe Atlas lets non-US founders incorporate a US LLC or C-corp remotely, getting access to US payment processing and US bank accounts. The cost is around five hundred dollars plus annual state fees. For non-US freelancers wanting easier US client billing, this can be the cleanest setup.

Common cross-border mistakes. Charging VAT to a foreign B2B client when you should not, missing tax treaty benefits and overpaying foreign withholding, forgetting to convert foreign currency at the IRS rate, neglecting to report foreign bank accounts on FinCEN Form 114 if balances exceed ten thousand dollars at any time during the year.

FBAR and FATCA reporting. US persons with foreign financial accounts must file FinCEN Form 114, the FBAR, if aggregate balances exceed ten thousand dollars at any time during the year. Form 8938 under FATCA may also apply. Penalties for nonfiling are severe. The Treasury Department BSA E-Filing System handles FBAR submissions online and is free.

Permanent establishment risk. If you spend significant time working in a foreign country, you may create a permanent establishment there, triggering local income tax filings. Most US tax treaties define PE thresholds. Generally working short trips of a few weeks does not create PE, but extended stays of months can. Digital nomads need to track days carefully.

Foreign earned income exclusion for digital nomads. Under IRC section 911, US citizens working abroad can exclude up to one hundred twenty-six thousand dollars of foreign earned income from federal income tax for two thousand twenty-six. Two qualifying tests, the bona fide residence test or the physical presence test, three hundred thirty days outside the US in any twelve-month period.

FEIE pitfalls. The exclusion only covers federal income tax, not self-employment tax. So a digital nomad freelancer still owes the full fifteen point three percent SE tax. Some nomads incorporate offshore to avoid SE tax, but anti-deferral rules under subpart F and GILTI under IRC section 951A often capture the income anyway.

Practical setup for cross-border freelancing. Mercury or Bluevine for US business banking, Wise for multi-currency holding and conversion, Stripe for payment processing, QuickBooks Online for accounting with multi-currency support, a CPA experienced in international issues for annual returns. The all-in monthly cost runs around eighty to one hundred fifty dollars and pays for itself in saved tax.

When to actually consult an international tax specialist. If you have clients in three or more countries, if you spend more than ninety days in any single foreign country, if you receive foreign withholding on payments, if you are considering offshore incorporation. Each of these triggers complexity that requires real expertise. Specialists charge three to six hundred an hour but the savings often dwarf the fee.

UK freelancer cross-border specifics. UK self-employed individuals invoicing EU clients use reverse charge for B2B services. UK freelancers with US clients should provide a Form W-8BEN to claim US-UK treaty benefits, generally reducing US withholding to zero on B2B service income. The UK-US treaty is one of the most favorable in the world for service freelancers.

Documentation that survives an audit. Every invoice with VAT or treaty notes clearly stated, every wire transfer record showing payment received in original currency and converted amount, every Form W-8BEN or W-9 exchanged with foreign clients, every foreign tax credit claim with supporting foreign tax receipts. Cross-border tax audits are document-intensive.

Subscribe to FinanzasClara and hit the bell. What's coming next week, the actual paperwork a US freelancer assembled to claim foreign tax credits across four countries in one tax year, including the timing strategy that minimized cash flow disruption. Drop a comment with the country giving you the most cross-border headache.
